Used method = 'Exact' method = 'MC' (inner loop).  Default 10000. method character string specifying computation method. Must 'Exact' (default) 'MC'. 'Exact' uses full enumeration possible multinomial count combinations (two-stage approach described Details). 'MC' samples nsim \((x_t, x_c)\) pairs via rmultinom, deduplicates \(K\) unique pairs (\(K \ll\) nsim), calls pbayespostpred2bin unique pair obtain Go/NoGo probabilities, weights decisions observed pair frequencies.  reuses validated probability logic Exact method, avoiding duplication computation. 'MC' method trades Monte Carlo variance substantially reduced computation time n1 /n2 large. error_if_Miss logical scalar; TRUE (default), function stops error Miss probability positive, prompting reconsideration thresholds. Gray_inc_Miss logical scalar; TRUE, Miss probability added Gray.  FALSE (default), Miss reported separately.  Active error_if_Miss = FALSE.

title: Go/NoGo/Gray Decision Probabilities for a Clinical Trial with Two Binary Endpoints — pbayesdecisionprob2bin
text: data frame one row per scenario columns: pi_t1 True treatment response probability Endpoint 1. pi_t2 True treatment response probability Endpoint 2. rho_t True within-arm correlation treatment arm. pi_c1 True control response probability Endpoint 1 (omitted design = 'uncontrolled'). pi_c2 True control response probability Endpoint 2 (omitted design = 'uncontrolled'). rho_c True within-arm correlation control arm (omitted design = 'uncontrolled'). Go Probability making Go decision. Gray Probability making Gray (inconclusive) decision. NoGo Probability making NoGo decision. Miss Probability Go NoGo criteria simultaneously met.  Included error_if_Miss = FALSE Gray_inc_Miss = FALSE. returned object S3 class pbayesdecisionprob2bin associated print method.

title: Go/NoGo/Gray Decision Probabilities for a Clinical Trial with Two Binary Endpoints — pbayesdecisionprob2bin
text: Computation proceeds two stages efficiency.  Stage 1, posterior predictive probabilities precomputed every possible multinomial count combination \((x_t, x_c)\).  Stage 2, operating characteristics scenario obtained weighting Stage 1 decisions multinomial probabilities true parameters, avoiding repeated Monte Carlo sampling per scenario. Two-stage computation. Stage 1 (precomputation): possible multinomial count combinations \(x_t\) enumerated using allmultinom. design = 'controlled' 'external', combinations \(x_c\) also enumerated Go/NoGo probability matrix PrGo[, j] dimensions \(n_t \times n_c\). design = 'uncontrolled', control distribution fixed \(\mathrm{Dir}(\alpha_{2,**} + z_{**})\) depend observed control counts; probability matrix therefore dimensions \(n_t \times 1\), eliminating \(\binom{n_2+3}{3}\)-row control enumeration associated Gamma sampling. stage independent true scenario parameters. Stage 2 (scenario evaluation): scenario \((\pi_{t1}, \pi_{t2}, \rho_t, \pi_{c1}, \pi_{c2}, \rho_c)\), getjointbin converts marginal rates correlation four-cell probability vector \(p_k\).  multinomial probability mass function weights Stage 1 decisions: $$\Pr(\mathrm{Go} \mid \Theta) = \sum_{,j}       \mathbf{1}(\mathrm{Go}_{ij}) \cdot       P(x_{t,} \mid n_1, p_t) \cdot P(x_{c,j} \mid n_2, p_c),$$ \(\mathbf{1}(\mathrm{Go}_{ij})\) Go indicator Stage 1. Stage 2 requires additional Monte Carlo fast even large scenario grids. Decision categories. Go: sum Go region probabilities \(\ge \gamma_1\) sum NoGo region probabilities \(< \gamma_2\). NoGo: sum Go region probabilities \(< \gamma_1\) sum NoGo region probabilities \(\ge \gamma_2\). Miss: Go NoGo criteria satisfied simultaneously. Gray: neither Go NoGo criterion satisfied.

what: Go/NoGo/Gray Decision Probabilities for Two Continuous Endpoints — pbayesdecisionprob2cont
title: Go/NoGo/Gray Decision Probabilities for Two Continuous Endpoints — pbayesdecisionprob2cont
text: Computes operating characteristics (Go, NoGo, Gray, optionally Miss probabilities) two-continuous-endpoint Bayesian Go/NoGo decision framework Monte Carlo simulation treatment scenarios.

title: Go/NoGo/Gray Decision Probabilities for Two Continuous Endpoints — pbayesdecisionprob2cont
code: pbayesdecisionprob2cont(   nsim,   prob,   design,   prior,   GoRegions,   NoGoRegions,   gamma1,   gamma2,   theta.TV1 = NULL,   theta.MAV1 = NULL,   theta.TV2 = NULL,   theta.MAV2 = NULL,   theta.NULL1 = NULL,   theta.NULL2 = NULL,   n1,   n2 = NULL,   m1 = NULL,   m2 = NULL,   mu1,   Sigma1,   mu2,   Sigma2,   kappa01 = NULL,   nu01 = NULL,   mu01 = NULL,   Lambda01 = NULL,   kappa02 = NULL,   nu02 = NULL,   mu02 = NULL,   Lambda02 = NULL,   r = NULL,   ne1 = NULL,   ne2 = NULL,   alpha01e = NULL,   alpha02e = NULL,   ybar_e1 = NULL,   ybar_e2 = NULL,   Se1 = NULL,   Se2 = NULL,   nMC = NULL,   method = "MC",   error_if_Miss = TRUE,   Gray_inc_Miss = FALSE,   seed )

title: Go/NoGo/Gray Decision Probabilities for Two Continuous Endpoints — pbayesdecisionprob2cont
text: nsim positive integer. Number simulated datasets per scenario. prob Character scalar: 'posterior' 'predictive'. design Character scalar: 'controlled', 'uncontrolled', 'external'. prior Character scalar: 'vague' 'N-Inv-Wishart'. GoRegions Integer vector. Region indices (1–9 posterior, 1–4 predictive) trigger Go decision. NoGoRegions Integer vector. Region indices trigger NoGo decision. Must disjoint GoRegions. gamma1 Numeric scalar (0, 1). Go threshold: Go \(P(\mathrm{GoRegions}) \ge \gamma_1\). gamma2 Numeric scalar (0, 1). NoGo threshold: NoGo \(P(\mathrm{NoGoRegions}) \ge \gamma_2\). theta.TV1 Numeric scalar NULL. Target value Endpoint 1 (required prob = 'posterior'). theta.MAV1 Numeric scalar NULL. Minimum acceptable value Endpoint 1 (required prob = 'posterior'). theta.TV2 Numeric scalar NULL. Target value Endpoint 2 (required prob = 'posterior'). theta.MAV2 Numeric scalar NULL. Minimum acceptable value Endpoint 2 (required prob = 'posterior'). theta.NULL1 Numeric scalar NULL. Null value Endpoint 1 (required prob = 'predictive'). theta.NULL2 Numeric scalar NULL. Null value Endpoint 2 (required prob = 'predictive'). n1 Positive integer. Treatment arm sample size. n2 Positive integer NULL. Control arm sample size (required design 'controlled' 'external'). m1 Positive integer NULL. Future treatment arm size (required prob = 'predictive'). m2 Positive integer NULL. Future control arm size (required prob = 'predictive'). mu1 Numeric matrix 2 columns. row gives true treatment mean vector one scenario.  length-2 vector coerced 1-row matrix. Sigma1 2x2 positive-definite matrix. True treatment covariance. mu2 Numeric matrix 2 columns length-2 vector. True control (hypothetical control) mean vector(s). Sigma2 2x2 positive-definite matrix. True control covariance. kappa01 Positive numeric NULL. NIW prior pseudo sample size treatment (required prior = 'N-Inv-Wishart'). nu01 Positive numeric NULL. NIW prior degrees freedom treatment (required prior = 'N-Inv-Wishart'). mu01 Length-2 numeric vector NULL. NIW prior mean treatment (required prior = 'N-Inv-Wishart'). Lambda01 2x2 numeric matrix NULL. NIW prior scale matrix treatment (required prior = 'N-Inv-Wishart'). kappa02 Positive numeric NULL. NIW prior pseudo sample size control (required design 'controlled' 'external' prior = 'N-Inv-Wishart'). nu02 Positive numeric NULL. NIW prior degrees freedom control. mu02 Length-2 numeric vector NULL. NIW prior mean control, hypothetical control mean design = 'uncontrolled'. Lambda02 2x2 numeric matrix NULL. NIW prior scale matrix control. r Positive numeric NULL. Variance scaling ratio hypothetical control arm (required design = 'uncontrolled'). ne1 Positive integer NULL. External treatment sample size (used design = 'external'). ne2 Positive integer NULL. External control sample size (used design = 'external'). alpha01e Numeric scalar (0, 1]. Power prior weight external treatment data. alpha02e Numeric scalar (0, 1]. Power prior weight external control data. ybar_e1 Length-2 numeric vector. External treatment sample mean. ybar_e2 Length-2 numeric vector. External control sample mean. Se1 2x2 numeric matrix. External treatment sum--squares matrix. Se2 2x2 numeric matrix. External control sum--squares matrix. nMC Positive integer NULL. Number Monte Carlo draws passed pbayespostpred2cont compute region probabilities simulated dataset.  Required method = 'MC'; may set NULL method = 'MM' (MM method uses analytical mvtnorm::pmvt require MC draws). Default NULL. method Character scalar: 'MC' 'MM' (Moment-Matching via mvtnorm::pmvt). Default 'MC'. error_if_Miss Logical scalar. TRUE (default), function stops scenario yields positive Miss probability. Gray_inc_Miss Logical scalar. TRUE, Miss probability folded Gray. FALSE (default), Miss reported separately. Active error_if_Miss = FALSE. seed numeric value reproducible random number generation.

title: Go/NoGo/Gray Decision Probabilities for Two Continuous Endpoints — pbayesdecisionprob2cont
text: data.frame one row per scenario columns mu1_ep1, mu1_ep2 (mu2_ep1, mu2_ep2 controlled/external), Go, Gray, NoGo, optionally Miss.  object S3 class c("pbayesdecisionprob2cont", "data.frame") input parameters attached attributes.

title: Go/NoGo/Gray Decision Probabilities for Two Continuous Endpoints — pbayesdecisionprob2cont
text: function follows structure pbayesdecisionprob1cont: scenario \(s\), nsim datasets simulated generating treatment (control) observations \(N_2(\mu_k^{(s)}, \Sigma_k)\).  minimise overhead, raw standardised residuals generated (scenario- invariant) shifted scenario mean. nsim simulated sufficient statistics \((\bar{y}_{1,}, S_{1,})\) (\((\bar{y}_{2,}, S_{2,})\) controlled/external designs) passed pbayespostpred2cont single vectorised call, returning \(nsim \times n_{\rm regions}\) matrix region probabilities. Go/NoGo/Miss probabilities obtained column means indicator matrices derived region probability matrix.

what: Bayesian Posterior or Posterior Predictive Probability for a Single Binary Endpoint — pbayespostpred1bin
title: Bayesian Posterior or Posterior Predictive Probability for a Single Binary Endpoint — pbayespostpred1bin
text: Computes Bayesian posterior probability posterior predictive probability binary-outcome clinical trials beta-binomial conjugate model. function supports controlled, uncontrolled, external-control designs, optional incorporation external data power priors. Vector inputs y1 y2 supported efficient batch processing (e.g., across possible trial outcomes pbayesdecisionprob1bin).

title: Bayesian Posterior or Posterior Predictive Probability for a Single Binary Endpoint — pbayespostpred1bin
code: pbayespostpred1bin(   prob = "posterior",   design = "controlled",   theta0,   n1,   n2,   y1,   y2,   a1,   a2,   b1,   b2,   m1,   m2,   z = NULL,   ne1,   ne2,   ye1,   ye2,   ae1,   ae2,   lower.tail = TRUE )

title: Bayesian Posterior or Posterior Predictive Probability for a Single Binary Endpoint — pbayespostpred1bin
text: prob character string specifying probability type. Must 'posterior' 'predictive'. design character string specifying trial design. Must 'controlled', 'uncontrolled', 'external'. theta0 numeric scalar (-1, 1) giving pre-specified threshold treatment effect (difference response rates). n1 positive integer giving number patients group 1 (treatment) proof--concept (PoC) trial. n2 positive integer giving number patients group 2 PoC trial. design = 'uncontrolled', hypothetical control sample size (required consistency designs). y1 non-negative integer integer vector giving observed number responders group 1 (must satisfy 0 <= y1 <= n1). y2 non-negative integer integer vector giving number responders group 2 (must satisfy 0 <= y2 <= n2). Set NULL design = 'uncontrolled' use z instead. provided vector, must length y1. a1 positive numeric scalar giving first shape parameter (alpha) prior Beta distribution group 1. a2 positive numeric scalar giving first shape parameter (alpha) prior Beta distribution group 2. b1 positive numeric scalar giving second shape parameter (beta) prior Beta distribution group 1. b2 positive numeric scalar giving second shape parameter (beta) prior Beta distribution group 2. m1 positive integer giving number patients group 1 future trial. Required prob = 'predictive'; otherwise set NULL. m2 positive integer giving number patients group 2 future trial. Required prob = 'predictive'; otherwise set NULL. z non-negative integer giving hypothetical number responders control group. Required design = 'uncontrolled'; otherwise set NULL. used, y2 NULL. ne1 positive integer giving number patients group 1 external data set. Required design = 'external' external treatment data available; otherwise set NULL. ne2 positive integer giving number patients group 2 external data set. Required design = 'external' external control data available; otherwise set NULL. ye1 non-negative integer giving number responders group 1 external data set. Required design = 'external'; otherwise set NULL. ye2 non-negative integer giving number responders group 2 external data set. Required design = 'external'; otherwise set NULL. ae1 numeric scalar (0, 1] giving power prior weight group 1. Controls degree borrowing: 1 = full borrowing. Required design = 'external'; otherwise set NULL. ae2 numeric scalar (0, 1] giving power prior weight group 2. Required design = 'external'; otherwise set NULL. lower.tail logical scalar; TRUE (default), function returns \(P(\mathrm{effect} \le \theta_0)\), otherwise \(P(\mathrm{effect} > \theta_0)\).

title: Bayesian Posterior or Posterior Predictive Probability for a Single Binary Endpoint — pbayespostpred1bin
text: numeric scalar vector [0, 1].  y1 y2 vectors length \(n\), vector length \(n\) returned.

title: Bayesian Posterior or Posterior Predictive Probability for a Single Binary Endpoint — pbayespostpred1bin
text: Posterior shape parameters computed beta-binomial conjugate model: Prior: \(\pi_j \sim \mathrm{Beta}(a_j,\, b_j)\). Posterior: \(\pi_j \mid y_j \sim         \mathrm{Beta}(a_j + y_j,\; b_j + n_j - y_j)\). design = 'external', external data incorporated power prior, inflates prior weighted external sufficient statistics: $$(\alpha_j^*, \beta_j^*) =   (a_j + ae_j \cdot ye_j,\; b_j + ae_j \cdot (ne_j - ye_j)).$$ design = 'uncontrolled', hypothetical control value z used place y2, n2 acts hypothetical control sample size. final probability obtained calling pbetadiff prob = 'posterior' pbetabinomdiff prob = 'predictive', applied element-wise via mapply.

title: Bayesian Posterior or Posterior Predictive Probability for a Single Binary Endpoint — pbayespostpred1bin
code: # Example 1: Controlled design - posterior probability (scalar) pbayespostpred1bin(   prob = 'posterior', design = 'controlled', theta0 = 0.15,   n1 = 12, n2 = 15, y1 = 7, y2 = 5,   a1 = 0.5, a2 = 0.5, b1 = 0.5, b2 = 0.5,   m1 = NULL, m2 = NULL, z = NULL,   ne1 = NULL, ne2 = NULL, ye1 = NULL, ye2 = NULL, ae1 = NULL, ae2 = NULL,   lower.tail = FALSE ) #> [1] 0.6860634  # Example 2: Controlled design - vectorised over outcomes pbayespostpred1bin(   prob = 'posterior', design = 'controlled', theta0 = 0.15,   n1 = 12, n2 = 15, y1 = c(5, 6, 7, 8), y2 = c(3, 4, 5, 6),   a1 = 0.5, a2 = 0.5, b1 = 0.5, b2 = 0.5,   m1 = NULL, m2 = NULL, z = NULL,   ne1 = NULL, ne2 = NULL, ye1 = NULL, ye2 = NULL, ae1 = NULL, ae2 = NULL,   lower.tail = FALSE ) #> [1] 0.6290637 0.6576595 0.6860634 0.7160059  # Example 3: Uncontrolled design - hypothetical control via z pbayespostpred1bin(   prob = 'posterior', design = 'uncontrolled', theta0 = 0.20,   n1 = 20, n2 = 20, y1 = 12, y2 = NULL,   a1 = 0.5, a2 = 0.5, b1 = 0.5, b2 = 0.5,   m1 = NULL, m2 = NULL, z = 3,   ne1 = NULL, ne2 = NULL, ye1 = NULL, ye2 = NULL, ae1 = NULL, ae2 = NULL,   lower.tail = FALSE ) #> [1] 0.952403  # Example 4: Controlled design - posterior predictive probability pbayespostpred1bin(   prob = 'predictive', design = 'controlled', theta0 = 0.10,   n1 = 12, n2 = 15, y1 = 7, y2 = 5,   a1 = 0.5, a2 = 0.5, b1 = 0.5, b2 = 0.5,   m1 = 30, m2 = 30, z = NULL,   ne1 = NULL, ne2 = NULL, ye1 = NULL, ye2 = NULL, ae1 = NULL, ae2 = NULL,   lower.tail = FALSE ) #> [1] 0.710533  # Example 5: External design - power prior with 50 percent borrowing pbayespostpred1bin(   prob = 'posterior', design = 'external', theta0 = 0.15,   n1 = 12, n2 = 15, y1 = 7, y2 = 9,   a1 = 0.5, a2 = 0.5, b1 = 0.5, b2 = 0.5,   m1 = NULL, m2 = NULL, z = NULL,   ne1 = 12, ne2 = 12, ye1 = 6, ye2 = 6, ae1 = 0.5, ae2 = 0.5,   lower.tail = FALSE ) #> [1] 0.1399401

what: Bayesian Posterior or Posterior Predictive Probability for a Clinical Trial with a Single Continuous Endpoint — pbayespostpred1cont
title: Bayesian Posterior or Posterior Predictive Probability for a Clinical Trial with a Single Continuous Endpoint — pbayespostpred1cont
text: Computes Bayesian posterior probability posterior predictive probability continuous-outcome clinical trials Normal-Inverse-Chi-squared (vague Jeffreys) conjugate model. function supports controlled, uncontrolled, external-control designs, optional incorporation external data power priors. Vector inputs bar.y1, s1, bar.y2, s2 supported efficient batch processing (e.g., across simulation replicates pbayesdecisionprob1cont).

title: Bayesian Posterior or Posterior Predictive Probability for a Clinical Trial with a Single Continuous Endpoint — pbayespostpred1cont
code: pbayespostpred1cont(   prob = "posterior",   design = "controlled",   prior = "vague",   CalcMethod = "NI",   theta0,   nMC = NULL,   n1,   n2,   m1 = NULL,   m2 = NULL,   kappa01 = NULL,   kappa02 = NULL,   nu01 = NULL,   nu02 = NULL,   mu01 = NULL,   mu02 = NULL,   sigma01 = NULL,   sigma02 = NULL,   bar.y1,   bar.y2 = NULL,   s1,   s2 = NULL,   r = NULL,   ne1 = NULL,   ne2 = NULL,   alpha01 = NULL,   alpha02 = NULL,   bar.ye1 = NULL,   bar.ye2 = NULL,   se1 = NULL,   se2 = NULL,   lower.tail = TRUE )

title: Bayesian Posterior or Posterior Predictive Probability for a Clinical Trial with a Single Continuous Endpoint — pbayespostpred1cont
text: prob character string specifying probability type. Must 'posterior' 'predictive'. design character string specifying trial design. Must 'controlled', 'uncontrolled', 'external'. prior character string specifying prior type. Must 'vague' (Jeffreys) 'N-Inv-Chisq' (Normal-Inverse-Chi-squared conjugate). CalcMethod character string specifying calculation method. Must 'NI' (numerical integration), 'MC' (Monte Carlo), 'MM' (Moment-Matching approximation). large nsim multi-scenario use, 'MM' strongly recommended; see Details. theta0 numeric scalar giving threshold treatment effect (difference means). nMC positive integer giving number Monte Carlo draws. Required CalcMethod = 'MC'; otherwise NULL. n1 positive integer giving number patients group 1 (treatment) proof--concept (PoC) trial. n2 positive integer giving number patients group 2 PoC trial. design = 'uncontrolled', set equal n1 (used calculations required consistency). m1 positive integer giving future sample size group 1. Required prob = 'predictive'; otherwise NULL. m2 positive integer giving future sample size group 2. Required prob = 'predictive'; otherwise NULL. kappa01 positive numeric scalar giving prior precision parameter group 1. Required prior = 'N-Inv-Chisq'; otherwise NULL. kappa02 positive numeric scalar giving prior precision parameter group 2. Required prior = 'N-Inv-Chisq' design = 'controlled'; otherwise NULL. nu01 positive numeric scalar giving prior degrees freedom group 1. Required prior = 'N-Inv-Chisq'; otherwise NULL. nu02 positive numeric scalar giving prior degrees freedom group 2. Required prior = 'N-Inv-Chisq' design = 'controlled'; otherwise NULL. mu01 numeric scalar giving prior mean group 1. Required prior = 'N-Inv-Chisq'; otherwise NULL. mu02 numeric scalar giving prior mean group 2 (design = 'controlled' prior = 'N-Inv-Chisq') hypothetical control mean (design = 'uncontrolled'). Required uncontrolled design; otherwise NULL. sigma01 positive numeric scalar giving prior scale group 1. Required prior = 'N-Inv-Chisq'; otherwise NULL. sigma02 positive numeric scalar giving prior scale group 2. Required prior = 'N-Inv-Chisq' design = 'controlled'; otherwise NULL. bar.y1 numeric scalar vector giving sample mean group 1. vector length nsim supplied, posterior parameters computed simultaneously replicates. bar.y2 numeric scalar vector giving sample mean group 2. Required design = 'controlled' 'external'; set NULL uncontrolled design. s1 positive numeric scalar vector giving sample standard deviation group 1. s2 positive numeric scalar vector giving sample standard deviation group 2. Required design = 'controlled' 'external'; otherwise NULL. r positive numeric scalar giving variance scaling factor hypothetical control. Required design = 'uncontrolled'; otherwise NULL. hypothetical control scale \(\mathrm{sd.control} = \sqrt{r} \cdot \mathrm{sd.treatment}\). ne1 positive integer giving external group 1 sample size. Required design = 'external' external treatment data; otherwise NULL. ne2 positive integer giving external group 2 sample size. Required design = 'external' external control data; otherwise NULL. alpha01 numeric scalar (0, 1] giving power prior weight group 1. Required ne1 provided; otherwise NULL. alpha02 numeric scalar (0, 1] giving power prior weight group 2. Required ne2 provided; otherwise NULL. bar.ye1 numeric scalar giving external group 1 sample mean. Required ne1 provided; otherwise NULL. bar.ye2 numeric scalar giving external group 2 sample mean. Required ne2 provided; otherwise NULL. se1 positive numeric scalar giving external group 1 sample SD. Required ne1 provided; otherwise NULL. se2 positive numeric scalar giving external group 2 sample SD. Required ne2 provided; otherwise NULL. lower.tail logical scalar; TRUE (default), returns \(P(\mathrm{effect} \le \theta_0)\), otherwise \(P(\mathrm{effect} > \theta_0)\).

title: Bayesian Posterior or Posterior Predictive Probability for a Clinical Trial with a Single Continuous Endpoint — pbayespostpred1cont
text: numeric scalar vector [0, 1].  input data parameters (bar.y1, etc.) vectors length \(n\), vector length \(n\) returned.

title: Bayesian Posterior or Posterior Predictive Probability for a Clinical Trial with a Single Continuous Endpoint — pbayespostpred1cont
text: Normal-Inverse-Chi-squared (N-Inv-ChiSq) conjugate model, marginal posterior/predictive distribution group mean follows non-standardised t-distribution, parameterised location \(\mu_k\), scale \(\sigma_k\), degrees freedom \(\nu_k\) depend design probability type. final probability computed one three methods: NI: exact numerical integration via ptdiff_NI. MC: Monte Carlo simulation via ptdiff_MC. MM: Moment-Matching approximation via ptdiff_MM. Performance note: CalcMethod = 'NI' calls integrate() per element input vector, prohibitively slow large vectors (e.g., nsim x n_scenarios). CalcMethod = 'MC' creates nMC x n matrix internally, consuming \(O(nMC \cdot n)\) memory. large-scale simulation use CalcMethod = 'MM', fully vectorised exact normal limit.

title: Bayesian Posterior or Posterior Predictive Probability for a Clinical Trial with a Single Continuous Endpoint — pbayespostpred1cont
code: # Example 1: Controlled design - posterior probability with N-Inv-Chisq prior pbayespostpred1cont(   prob = 'posterior', design = 'controlled', prior = 'N-Inv-Chisq', CalcMethod = 'NI',   theta0 = 2, n1 = 12, n2 = 12,   kappa01 = 5, kappa02 = 5, nu01 = 5, nu02 = 5,   mu01 = 5, mu02 = 5, sigma01 = sqrt(5), sigma02 = sqrt(5),   bar.y1 = 2, bar.y2 = 0, s1 = 1, s2 = 1,   m1 = NULL, m2 = NULL, r = NULL,   ne1 = NULL, ne2 = NULL, alpha01 = NULL, alpha02 = NULL,   bar.ye1 = NULL, bar.ye2 = NULL, se1 = NULL, se2 = NULL,   lower.tail = FALSE ) #> [1] 0.2438672  # Example 2: Uncontrolled design with hypothetical control and vague prior pbayespostpred1cont(   prob = 'posterior', design = 'uncontrolled', prior = 'vague', CalcMethod = 'MM',   theta0 = 1.5, n1 = 15, n2 = 15,   bar.y1 = 3.5, bar.y2 = NULL, s1 = 1.2, s2 = NULL,   mu02 = 1.5, r = 1.2,   kappa01 = NULL, kappa02 = NULL, nu01 = NULL, nu02 = NULL,   mu01 = NULL, sigma01 = NULL, sigma02 = NULL,   m1 = NULL, m2 = NULL,   ne1 = NULL, ne2 = NULL, alpha01 = NULL, alpha02 = NULL,   bar.ye1 = NULL, bar.ye2 = NULL, se1 = NULL, se2 = NULL,   lower.tail = FALSE ) #> [1] 0.8484152  # Example 3: Posterior predictive probability - controlled design pbayespostpred1cont(   prob = 'predictive', design = 'controlled', prior = 'N-Inv-Chisq', CalcMethod = 'MM',   theta0 = 0.5, n1 = 15, n2 = 15, m1 = 100, m2 = 100,   kappa01 = 3, kappa02 = 3, nu01 = 4, nu02 = 4,   mu01 = 2, mu02 = 2, sigma01 = 2, sigma02 = 2,   bar.y1 = 2.5, bar.y2 = 1.8, s1 = 1.8, s2 = 1.6,   r = NULL, ne1 = NULL, ne2 = NULL, alpha01 = NULL, alpha02 = NULL,   bar.ye1 = NULL, bar.ye2 = NULL, se1 = NULL, se2 = NULL,   lower.tail = FALSE ) #> [1] 0.62567  # Example 4: External control design with power prior pbayespostpred1cont(   prob = 'posterior', design = 'external', prior = 'vague', CalcMethod = 'NI',   theta0 = 1.5, n1 = 12, n2 = 12,   bar.y1 = 4, bar.y2 = 2, s1 = 1.2, s2 = 1.1,   ne2 = 20, alpha02 = 0.5, bar.ye2 = 1.8, se2 = 1.0,   kappa01 = NULL, kappa02 = NULL, nu01 = NULL, nu02 = NULL,   mu01 = NULL, mu02 = NULL, sigma01 = NULL, sigma02 = NULL,   m1 = NULL, m2 = NULL, r = NULL,   ne1 = NULL, alpha01 = NULL, bar.ye1 = NULL, se1 = NULL,   lower.tail = FALSE ) #> [1] 0.9116773  # Example 5: Vectorised usage across simulation replicates set.seed(1) bar.y1_vec <- rnorm(100, mean = 3, sd = 1 / sqrt(12)) s1_vec     <- sqrt(rchisq(100, df = 11) * 1 / 11) bar.y2_vec <- rnorm(100, mean = 1, sd = 1 / sqrt(12)) s2_vec     <- sqrt(rchisq(100, df = 11) * 1 / 11) pbayespostpred1cont(   prob = 'posterior', design = 'controlled', prior = 'vague', CalcMethod = 'MM',   theta0 = 1.5, n1 = 12, n2 = 12,   bar.y1 = bar.y1_vec, bar.y2 = bar.y2_vec, s1 = s1_vec, s2 = s2_vec,   kappa01 = NULL, kappa02 = NULL, nu01 = NULL, nu02 = NULL,   mu01 = NULL, mu02 = NULL, sigma01 = NULL, sigma02 = NULL,   m1 = NULL, m2 = NULL, r = NULL,   ne1 = NULL, ne2 = NULL, alpha01 = NULL, alpha02 = NULL,   bar.ye1 = NULL, bar.ye2 = NULL, se1 = NULL, se2 = NULL,   lower.tail = FALSE ) #>   [1] 0.9991866 0.9627594 0.5972301 0.9850434 0.9307172 0.5880294 0.9864382 #>   [8] 0.7979074 0.9711989 0.6917204 0.9365251 0.9729759 0.9158296 0.1407004 #>  [15] 0.9988910 0.9009725 0.8977252 0.9457736 0.8659228 0.9398836 0.9226872 #>  [22] 0.9622810 0.9673058 0.1767523 0.7960044 0.9880035 0.7890139 0.4556129 #>  [29] 0.8656441 0.8030721 0.9919771 0.9392702 0.8103071 0.4864825 0.5229257 #>  [36] 0.8241322 0.9315695 0.8957300 0.9936097 0.9798690 0.7702987 0.9376857 #>  [43] 0.4389233 0.9221541 0.4788707 0.9556261 0.7874543 0.9922418 0.6659439 #>  [50] 0.9457863 0.9703745 0.4352707 0.9467480 0.7907318 0.9962790 0.9979437 #>  [57] 0.5905794 0.5918643 0.7388999 0.9214727 0.9865066 0.8700752 0.9878045 #>  [64] 0.4944146 0.7235091 0.7376461 0.1617060 0.9766214 0.9027928 0.9627555 #>  [71] 0.9875465 0.2955987 0.9358777 0.2158708 0.2081446 0.9098387 0.6919850 #>  [78] 0.9582525 0.8392311 0.5311681 0.7454596 0.9579094 0.9835332 0.5799441 #>  [85] 0.7665205 0.9565932 0.9854182 0.9863533 0.8094721 0.6944212 0.4079357 #>  [92] 0.9656720 0.9999672 0.8476460 0.9807729 0.9462620 0.5760098 0.8906253 #>  [99] 0.4653722 0.8410303

what: Bayesian Posterior or Posterior Predictive Probability for a Clinical Trial with Two Binary Endpoints — pbayespostpred2bin
title: Bayesian Posterior or Posterior Predictive Probability for a Clinical Trial with Two Binary Endpoints — pbayespostpred2bin
text: Computes Bayesian posterior probability posterior predictive probability clinical trials two binary endpoints Dirichlet-multinomial conjugate model.  function returns probabilities nine decision regions (posterior) four decision regions (predictive) defined target values (TV) minimum acceptable values (MAV) endpoints.  Three study designs supported: controlled, uncontrolled (hypothetical control), external-control (power prior).

title: Bayesian Posterior or Posterior Predictive Probability for a Clinical Trial with Two Binary Endpoints — pbayespostpred2bin
code: pbayespostpred2bin(   prob = "posterior",   design = "controlled",   theta.TV1 = NULL,   theta.MAV1 = NULL,   theta.TV2 = NULL,   theta.MAV2 = NULL,   theta.NULL1 = NULL,   theta.NULL2 = NULL,   x1_00,   x1_01,   x1_10,   x1_11,   x2_00 = NULL,   x2_01 = NULL,   x2_10 = NULL,   x2_11 = NULL,   a1_00,   a1_01,   a1_10,   a1_11,   a2_00,   a2_01,   a2_10,   a2_11,   m1 = NULL,   m2 = NULL,   z00 = NULL,   z01 = NULL,   z10 = NULL,   z11 = NULL,   xe1_00 = NULL,   xe1_01 = NULL,   xe1_10 = NULL,   xe1_11 = NULL,   xe2_00 = NULL,   xe2_01 = NULL,   xe2_10 = NULL,   xe2_11 = NULL,   ae1 = NULL,   ae2 = NULL,   nMC = 10000L )

title: Bayesian Posterior or Posterior Predictive Probability for a Clinical Trial with Two Binary Endpoints — pbayespostpred2bin
text: prob character string specifying probability type. Must 'posterior' 'predictive'. design character string specifying trial design. Must 'controlled', 'uncontrolled', 'external'. theta.TV1 numeric scalar giving target value (TV) threshold Endpoint 1.  Required prob = 'posterior'; must satisfy theta.TV1 > theta.MAV1.  Set NULL prob = 'predictive'. theta.MAV1 numeric scalar giving minimum acceptable value (MAV) threshold Endpoint 1.  Required prob = 'posterior'; must satisfy theta.TV1 > theta.MAV1.  Set NULL prob = 'predictive'. theta.TV2 numeric scalar giving target value (TV) threshold Endpoint 2.  Required prob = 'posterior'; must satisfy theta.TV2 > theta.MAV2.  Set NULL prob = 'predictive'. theta.MAV2 numeric scalar giving minimum acceptable value (MAV) threshold Endpoint 2.  Required prob = 'posterior'; must satisfy theta.TV2 > theta.MAV2.  Set NULL prob = 'predictive'. theta.NULL1 numeric scalar giving null hypothesis threshold Endpoint 1.  Required prob = 'predictive'; set NULL prob = 'posterior'. theta.NULL2 numeric scalar giving null hypothesis threshold Endpoint 2.  Required prob = 'predictive'; set NULL prob = 'posterior'. x1_00 non-negative integer giving count (0,0) responses group 1 (Endpoint 1 = 0, Endpoint 2 = 0). x1_01 non-negative integer giving count (0,1) responses group 1. x1_10 non-negative integer giving count (1,0) responses group 1. x1_11 non-negative integer giving count (1,1) responses group 1. x2_00 non-negative integer giving count (0,0) responses group 2.  used design = 'uncontrolled'; set NULL case. x2_01 non-negative integer giving count (0,1) responses group 2.  used design = 'uncontrolled'; set NULL case. x2_10 non-negative integer giving count (1,0) responses group 2.  used design = 'uncontrolled'; set NULL case. x2_11 non-negative integer giving count (1,1) responses group 2.  used design = 'uncontrolled'; set NULL case. a1_00 positive numeric scalar giving Dirichlet prior parameter (0,0) response pattern group 1. a1_01 positive numeric scalar giving Dirichlet prior parameter (0,1) response pattern group 1. a1_10 positive numeric scalar giving Dirichlet prior parameter (1,0) response pattern group 1. a1_11 positive numeric scalar giving Dirichlet prior parameter (1,1) response pattern group 1. a2_00 positive numeric scalar giving Dirichlet prior parameter (0,0) response pattern group 2.  design = 'uncontrolled', serves hyperparameter hypothetical control distribution (see Details). a2_01 positive numeric scalar giving Dirichlet prior parameter (0,1) response pattern group 2.  design = 'uncontrolled', serves hyperparameter hypothetical control distribution. a2_10 positive numeric scalar giving Dirichlet prior parameter (1,0) response pattern group 2.  design = 'uncontrolled', serves hyperparameter hypothetical control distribution. a2_11 positive numeric scalar giving Dirichlet prior parameter (1,1) response pattern group 2.  design = 'uncontrolled', serves hyperparameter hypothetical control distribution. m1 positive integer giving future sample size group 1. title: Bayesian Posterior or Posterior Predictive Probability for a Clinical Trial with Two Binary Endpoints — pbayespostpred2bin
text: named numeric vector region probabilities. prob = 'posterior': length 9, named R1, ..., R9, corresponding regions defined TV MAV thresholds endpoints (row-major order: Endpoint 1 varies slowest). prob = 'predictive': length 4, named R1, ..., R4. elements non-negative sum 1.

title: Bayesian Posterior or Posterior Predictive Probability for a Clinical Trial with Two Binary Endpoints — pbayespostpred2bin
text: Model. four response categories ordered (0,0), (0,1), (1,0), (1,1). arm \(k \\{1, 2\}\), observed count vector follows multinomial distribution, conjugate Dirichlet prior placed cell probability vector \(p_k\): $$p_k \sim \mathrm{Dir}(\alpha_{k,00},\, \alpha_{k,01},\,       \alpha_{k,10},\, \alpha_{k,11}).$$ posterior $$p_k \mid x_k \sim       \mathrm{Dir}(\alpha_{k,00} + x_{k,00},\; \alpha_{k,01} + x_{k,01},\;       \alpha_{k,10} + x_{k,10},\; \alpha_{k,11} + x_{k,11}).$$ Marginal response rates \(\pi_{k1} = p_{k,10} + p_{k,11}\) (Endpoint 1) \(\pi_{k2} = p_{k,01} + p_{k,11}\) (Endpoint 2). Treatment effects \(\theta_1 = \pi_{11} - \pi_{21}\) \(\theta_2 = \pi_{12} - \pi_{22}\). Uncontrolled design. design = 'uncontrolled', concurrent control data available.  hypothetical control distribution specified Dirichlet distribution directly via prior hyperparameters a2_00, a2_01, a2_10, a2_11, augmented hypothetical control counts z00, z01, z10, z11: $$p_2 \sim \mathrm{Dir}(\alpha_{2,00} + z_{00},\; \alpha_{2,01} +       z_{01},\; \alpha_{2,10} + z_{10},\; \alpha_{2,11} + z_{11}).$$ Monte Carlo samples distribution replace data-derived posterior samples group 2 subsequent computations. External design. design = 'external', power prior augments Dirichlet prior parameters: $$\alpha_{k,lm}^{*} = \alpha_{k,lm} + a_{0k} \cdot x_{e k,lm}.$$ Posterior probability regions (prob = 'posterior'). nine regions follow Table 1 reference (row-major order, Endpoint 1 varies slowest): R1: \(\theta_1 > TV_1\) \(\theta_2 > TV_2\) R2: \(\theta_1 > TV_1\) \(TV_2 \ge \theta_2 > MAV_2\) R3: \(\theta_1 > TV_1\) \(\theta_2 \le MAV_2\) R4: \(TV_1 \ge \theta_1 > MAV_1\) \(\theta_2 > TV_2\) R5: \(TV_1 \ge \theta_1 > MAV_1\) \(TV_2 \ge \theta_2 > MAV_2\) R6: \(TV_1 \ge \theta_1 > MAV_1\) \(\theta_2 \le MAV_2\) R7: \(\theta_1 \le MAV_1\) \(\theta_2 > TV_2\) R8: \(\theta_1 \le MAV_1\) \(TV_2 \ge \theta_2 > MAV_2\) R9: \(\theta_1 \le MAV_1\) \(\theta_2 \le MAV_2\) Predictive probability regions (prob = 'predictive'). four regions follow Table 2 reference: R1: \(\tilde\theta_1 > NULL_1\) \(\tilde\theta_2 > NULL_2\) R2: \(\tilde\theta_1 > NULL_1\) \(\tilde\theta_2 \le NULL_2\) R3: \(\tilde\theta_1 \le NULL_1\) \(\tilde\theta_2 > NULL_2\) R4: \(\tilde\theta_1 \le NULL_1\) \(\tilde\theta_2 \le NULL_2\)

title: Bayesian Posterior or Posterior Predictive Probability for a Clinical Trial with Two Binary Endpoints — pbayespostpred2bin
code: # Example 1: Posterior probability, controlled design pbayespostpred2bin(   prob = 'posterior', design = 'controlled',   theta.TV1 = 0.20, theta.MAV1 = 0.10,   theta.TV2 = 0.20, theta.MAV2 = 0.10,   theta.NULL1 = NULL, theta.NULL2 = NULL,   x1_00 = 5, x1_01 = 3, x1_10 = 4, x1_11 = 8,   x2_00 = 8, x2_01 = 4, x2_10 = 5, x2_11 = 3,   a1_00 = 0.5, a1_01 = 0.5, a1_10 = 0.5, a1_11 = 0.5,   a2_00 = 0.5, a2_01 = 0.5, a2_10 = 0.5, a2_11 = 0.5,   m1 = NULL, m2 = NULL,   z00 = NULL, z01 = NULL, z10 = NULL, z11 = NULL,   xe1_00 = NULL, xe1_01 = NULL, xe1_10 = NULL, xe1_11 = NULL,   xe2_00 = NULL, xe2_01 = NULL, xe2_10 = NULL, xe2_11 = NULL,   ae1 = NULL, ae2 = NULL,   nMC = 10000 ) #>     R1     R2     R3     R4     R5     R6     R7     R8     R9  #> 0.2303 0.1151 0.1080 0.1148 0.0684 0.0743 0.1143 0.0756 0.0992   # Example 2: Posterior predictive probability, controlled design pbayespostpred2bin(   prob = 'predictive', design = 'controlled',   theta.TV1 = NULL, theta.MAV1 = NULL,   theta.TV2 = NULL, theta.MAV2 = NULL,   theta.NULL1 = 0.15, theta.NULL2 = 0.15,   x1_00 = 3, x1_01 = 2, x1_10 = 3, x1_11 = 7,   x2_00 = 6, x2_01 = 3, x2_10 = 4, x2_11 = 2,   a1_00 = 1, a1_01 = 1, a1_10 = 1, a1_11 = 1,   a2_00 = 1, a2_01 = 1, a2_10 = 1, a2_11 = 1,   m1 = 50, m2 = 50,   z00 = NULL, z01 = NULL, z10 = NULL, z11 = NULL,   xe1_00 = NULL, xe1_01 = NULL, xe1_10 = NULL, xe1_11 = NULL,   xe2_00 = NULL, xe2_01 = NULL, xe2_10 = NULL, xe2_11 = NULL,   ae1 = NULL, ae2 = NULL,   nMC = 10000 ) #>     R1     R2     R3     R4  #> 0.4306 0.2062 0.2127 0.1505   # Example 3: Posterior probability, external control design pbayespostpred2bin(   prob = 'posterior', design = 'external',   theta.TV1 = 0.20, theta.MAV1 = 0.10,   theta.TV2 = 0.20, theta.MAV2 = 0.10,   theta.NULL1 = NULL, theta.NULL2 = NULL,   x1_00 = 5, x1_01 = 3, x1_10 = 4, x1_11 = 8,   x2_00 = 8, x2_01 = 4, x2_10 = 5, x2_11 = 3,   a1_00 = 0.5, a1_01 = 0.5, a1_10 = 0.5, a1_11 = 0.5,   a2_00 = 0.5, a2_01 = 0.5, a2_10 = 0.5, a2_11 = 0.5,   m1 = NULL, m2 = NULL,   z00 = NULL, z01 = NULL, z10 = NULL, z11 = NULL,   xe1_00 = NULL, xe1_01 = NULL, xe1_10 = NULL, xe1_11 = NULL,   xe2_00 = 3L, xe2_01 = 2L, xe2_10 = 3L, xe2_11 = 2L,   ae1 = NULL, ae2 = 0.5,   nMC = 10000 ) #>     R1     R2     R3     R4     R5     R6     R7     R8     R9  #> 0.2049 0.1069 0.0984 0.1210 0.0746 0.0856 0.1146 0.0854 0.1086

Model. endpoints follow bivariate Normal distribution \(y_{k,j} \sim N_2(\mu_k, \Sigma_k)\) arm \(k \{t, c\}\). treatment effect \(	heta = \mu_t - \mu_c\). Posterior distribution (vague prior). $$\mu_k | Y_k \sim t_{n_k - 2}\!\left( ar{y}_k,\;        rac{S_k}{n_k(n_k - 2)}ight)$$ Posterior distribution (NIW prior). $$\mu_k | Y_k \sim t_{
u_{nk} - 1}\!\left(\mu_{nk},\;        rac{\Lambda_{nk}}{\kappa_{nk}(
u_{nk} - 1)}ight)$$ updated hyperparameters \(\kappa_{nk} = \kappa_{0k} + n_k\), \(
u_{nk} = 
u_{0k} + n_k\), \(\mu_{nk} = (\kappa_{0k}\mu_{0k} + n_k ar{y}_k)/\kappa_{nk}\), \(\Lambda_{nk} = \Lambda_{0k} + S_k + \kappa_{0k}n_k( ar{y}_k - \mu_{0k})( ar{y}_k - \mu_{0k})^T / \kappa_{nk}\). Predictive distribution. scale matrix single future observation inflated \((1 + n_k)/n_k\) (vague) \((1 + \kappa_{nk})/\kappa_{nk}\) (NIW) relative posterior. mean \(m_k\) future observations scale divided \(m_k\). Posterior probability regions (prob = 'posterior'). Row-major 2x2 grid; Endpoint 1 varies slowest: R1: \(	ilde	heta_1 > 	heta_{m NULL1}\) \(	ilde	heta_2 > 	heta_{m NULL2}\) R2: \(	ilde	heta_1 > 	heta_{m NULL1}\) \(	ilde	heta_2 \le 	heta_{m NULL2}\) R3: \(	ilde	heta_1 \le 	heta_{m NULL1}\) \(	ilde	heta_2 > 	heta_{m NULL2}\) R4: \(	ilde	heta_1 \le 	heta_{m NULL1}\) \(	ilde	heta_2 \le 	heta_{m NULL2}\) Uncontrolled design. NIW prior: \(\mu_c \sim t_{
u_{nt} - 1}(\mu_{0c},\; r\Lambda_{nt} / [\kappa_{nt}(
u_{nt} - 1)])\). parameter r allows variance scale hypothetical control differ treatment arm. External design. Incorporated via power prior NIW conjugate representation. effective posterior hyperparameters obtained constructing power prior external data weight \(a_0\), updating current PoC data (see Conjugate_power_prior.pdf, Theorem 5). Vectorised usage. ybar1 supplied \(N 	imes 2\) matrix S1 list \(N\) scatter matrices, function computes region probabilities \(N\) observations single call, returning \(N 	imes n_{m regions}\) matrix.  method = 'MC', standard normal chi-squared variates pre-generated (size nMC) reused across observations, Cholesky factor replicate-specific scale matrix recomputed per observation.  method = 'MM', moment-matching parameters computed per observation (since depend replicate-specific \(V_k\)) mvtnorm::pmvt called per region per observation.

code: # Example 1: Posterior probability, controlled design, vague prior S1 <- matrix(c(18.0, 3.6, 3.6, 9.0), 2, 2) S2 <- matrix(c(16.0, 2.8, 2.8, 8.5), 2, 2) pbayespostpred2cont(   prob = 'posterior', design = 'controlled', prior = 'vague',   theta.TV1 = 1.5, theta.MAV1 = 0.5,   theta.TV2 = 1.0, theta.MAV2 = 0.3,   theta.NULL1 = NULL, theta.NULL2 = NULL,   n1 = 12L, n2 = 12L,   ybar1 = c(3.5, 2.1), S1 = S1,   ybar2 = c(1.8, 1.0), S2 = S2,   m1 = NULL, m2 = NULL,   kappa01 = NULL, nu01 = NULL, mu01 = NULL, Lambda01 = NULL,   kappa02 = NULL, nu02 = NULL, mu02 = NULL, Lambda02 = NULL,   r = NULL,   ne1 = NULL, ne2 = NULL, alpha01e = NULL, alpha02e = NULL,   ybar_e1 = NULL, ybar_e2 = NULL, Se1 = NULL, Se2 = NULL,   nMC = 1000L ) #>    R1    R2    R3    R4    R5    R6    R7    R8    R9  #> 0.432 0.209 0.012 0.153 0.160 0.016 0.009 0.009 0.000   # Example 2: Posterior probability, controlled design, NIW prior S1 <- matrix(c(18.0, 3.6, 3.6, 9.0), 2, 2) S2 <- matrix(c(16.0, 2.8, 2.8, 8.5), 2, 2) L0 <- matrix(c(20.0, 0.0, 0.0, 10.0), 2, 2) pbayespostpred2cont(   prob = 'posterior', design = 'controlled', prior = 'N-Inv-Wishart',   theta.TV1 = 1.5, theta.MAV1 = 0.5,   theta.TV2 = 1.0, theta.MAV2 = 0.3,   theta.NULL1 = NULL, theta.NULL2 = NULL,   n1 = 12L, n2 = 12L,   ybar1 = c(3.5, 2.1), S1 = S1,   ybar2 = c(1.8, 1.0), S2 = S2,   m1 = NULL, m2 = NULL,   kappa01 = 2.0, nu01 = 5.0, mu01 = c(2.0, 1.0), Lambda01 = L0,   kappa02 = 2.0, nu02 = 5.0, mu02 = c(1.0, 0.5), Lambda02 = L0,   r = NULL,   ne1 = NULL, ne2 = NULL, alpha01e = NULL, alpha02e = NULL,   ybar_e1 = NULL, ybar_e2 = NULL, Se1 = NULL, Se2 = NULL,   nMC = 1000L ) #>    R1    R2    R3    R4    R5    R6    R7    R8    R9  #> 0.331 0.222 0.027 0.177 0.190 0.023 0.009 0.015 0.006   # Example 3: Posterior probability, uncontrolled design, NIW prior # mu02 specifies the hypothetical control location; r scales the variance S1 <- matrix(c(18.0, 3.6, 3.6, 9.0), 2, 2) L0 <- matrix(c(20.0, 0.0, 0.0, 10.0), 2, 2) pbayespostpred2cont(   prob = 'posterior', design = 'uncontrolled', prior = 'N-Inv-Wishart',   theta.TV1 = 1.5, theta.MAV1 = 0.5,   theta.TV2 = 1.0, theta.MAV2 = 0.3,   theta.NULL1 = NULL, theta.NULL2 = NULL,   n1 = 12L, n2 = NULL,   ybar1 = c(3.5, 2.1), S1 = S1,   ybar2 = NULL, S2 = NULL,   m1 = NULL, m2 = NULL,   kappa01 = 2.0, nu01 = 5.0, mu01 = c(2.0, 1.0), Lambda01 = L0,   kappa02 = NULL, nu02 = NULL, mu02 = c(1.0, 0.5), Lambda02 = NULL,   r = 1.0,   ne1 = NULL, ne2 = NULL, alpha01e = NULL, alpha02e = NULL,   ybar_e1 = NULL, ybar_e2 = NULL, Se1 = NULL, Se2 = NULL,   nMC = 1000L ) #>    R1    R2    R3    R4    R5    R6    R7    R8    R9  #> 0.738 0.136 0.006 0.076 0.039 0.000 0.003 0.001 0.001   # Example 4: Posterior probability, external design (control only), NIW prior S1  <- matrix(c(18.0, 3.6, 3.6, 9.0), 2, 2) S2  <- matrix(c(16.0, 2.8, 2.8, 8.5), 2, 2) L0  <- matrix(c(20.0, 0.0, 0.0, 10.0), 2, 2) Se2 <- matrix(c(15.0, 2.5, 2.5, 7.5), 2, 2) pbayespostpred2cont(   prob = 'posterior', design = 'external', prior = 'N-Inv-Wishart',   theta.TV1 = 1.5, theta.MAV1 = 0.5,   theta.TV2 = 1.0, theta.MAV2 = 0.3,   theta.NULL1 = NULL, theta.NULL2 = NULL,   n1 = 12L, n2 = 12L,   ybar1 = c(3.5, 2.1), S1 = S1,   ybar2 = c(1.8, 1.0), S2 = S2,   m1 = NULL, m2 = NULL,   kappa01 = 2.0, nu01 = 5.0, mu01 = c(2.0, 1.0), Lambda01 = L0,   kappa02 = 2.0, nu02 = 5.0, mu02 = c(1.0, 0.5), Lambda02 = L0,   r = NULL,   ne1 = NULL, ne2 = 10L, alpha01e = NULL, alpha02e = 0.5,   ybar_e1 = NULL, ybar_e2 = c(1.5, 0.8), Se1 = NULL, Se2 = Se2,   nMC = 1000L ) #>    R1    R2    R3    R4    R5    R6    R7    R8    R9  #> 0.376 0.208 0.016 0.176 0.175 0.021 0.007 0.018 0.003   # Example 5: Predictive probability, controlled design, vague prior S1 <- matrix(c(18.0, 3.6, 3.6, 9.0), 2, 2) S2 <- matrix(c(16.0, 2.8, 2.8, 8.5), 2, 2) pbayespostpred2cont(   prob = 'predictive', design = 'controlled', prior = 'vague',   theta.TV1 = NULL, theta.MAV1 = NULL,   theta.TV2 = NULL, theta.MAV2 = NULL,   theta.NULL1 = 0.5, theta.NULL2 = 0.3,   n1 = 12L, n2 = 12L,   ybar1 = c(3.5, 2.1), S1 = S1,   ybar2 = c(1.8, 1.0), S2 = S2,   m1 = 30L, m2 = 30L,   kappa01 = NULL, nu01 = NULL, mu01 = NULL, Lambda01 = NULL,   kappa02 = NULL, nu02 = NULL, mu02 = NULL, Lambda02 = NULL,   r = NULL,   ne1 = NULL, ne2 = NULL, alpha01e = NULL, alpha02e = NULL,   ybar_e1 = NULL, ybar_e2 = NULL, Se1 = NULL, Se2 = NULL,   nMC = 1000L ) #>    R1    R2    R3    R4  #> 0.995 0.003 0.001 0.001   # Example 6: Posterior probability, controlled design, NIW prior, MM method S1 <- matrix(c(18.0, 3.6, 3.6, 9.0), 2, 2) S2 <- matrix(c(16.0, 2.8, 2.8, 8.5), 2, 2) L0 <- matrix(c(20.0, 0.0, 0.0, 10.0), 2, 2) pbayespostpred2cont(   prob = 'posterior', design = 'controlled', prior = 'N-Inv-Wishart',   theta.TV1 = 1.5, theta.MAV1 = 0.5,   theta.TV2 = 1.0, theta.MAV2 = 0.3,   theta.NULL1 = NULL, theta.NULL2 = NULL,   n1 = 12L, n2 = 12L,   ybar1 = c(3.5, 2.1), S1 = S1,   ybar2 = c(1.8, 1.0), S2 = S2,   m1 = NULL, m2 = NULL,   kappa01 = 2.0, nu01 = 5.0, mu01 = c(2.0, 1.0), Lambda01 = L0,   kappa02 = 2.0, nu02 = 5.0, mu02 = c(1.0, 0.5), Lambda02 = L0,   r = NULL,   ne1 = NULL, ne2 = NULL, alpha01e = NULL, alpha02e = NULL,   ybar_e1 = NULL, ybar_e2 = NULL, Se1 = NULL, Se2 = NULL,   nMC = 1000L, method = 'MM' ) #>          R1          R2          R3          R4          R5          R6  #> 0.317361008 0.223375689 0.023622983 0.180438104 0.186573213 0.027206352  #>          R7          R8          R9  #> 0.015175005 0.021079479 0.005168167   # Example 7: Vectorised call -- N = 3 observations simultaneously S1_list <- list(   matrix(c(18.0, 3.6, 3.6, 9.0), 2, 2),   matrix(c(20.0, 4.0, 4.0, 10.0), 2, 2),   matrix(c(15.0, 2.5, 2.5, 7.5), 2, 2) ) S2_list <- list(   matrix(c(16.0, 2.8, 2.8, 8.5), 2, 2),   matrix(c(17.0, 3.0, 3.0, 9.0), 2, 2),   matrix(c(14.0, 2.2, 2.2, 7.0), 2, 2) ) ybar1_mat <- rbind(c(3.5, 2.1), c(4.0, 2.5), c(2.5, 1.5)) ybar2_mat <- rbind(c(1.8, 1.0), c(1.9, 1.1), c(1.7, 0.9)) pbayespostpred2cont(   prob = 'posterior', design = 'controlled', prior = 'vague',   theta.TV1 = 1.5, theta.MAV1 = 0.5,   theta.TV2 = 1.0, theta.MAV2 = 0.3,   theta.NULL1 = NULL, theta.NULL2 = NULL,   n1 = 12L, n2 = 12L,   ybar1 = ybar1_mat, S1 = S1_list,   ybar2 = ybar2_mat, S2 = S2_list,   m1 = NULL, m2 = NULL,   kappa01 = NULL, nu01 = NULL, mu01 = NULL, Lambda01 = NULL,   kappa02 = NULL, nu02 = NULL, mu02 = NULL, Lambda02 = NULL,   r = NULL,   ne1 = NULL, ne2 = NULL, alpha01e = NULL, alpha02e = NULL,   ybar_e1 = NULL, ybar_e2 = NULL, Se1 = NULL, Se2 = NULL,   nMC = 1000L ) #>         R1    R2    R3    R4    R5    R6    R7    R8    R9 #> [1,] 0.415 0.214 0.009 0.174 0.138 0.026 0.009 0.013 0.002 #> [2,] 0.706 0.121 0.005 0.118 0.040 0.003 0.003 0.003 0.001 #> [3,] 0.027 0.058 0.010 0.095 0.407 0.112 0.024 0.183 0.084

what: Cumulative Distribution Function of the Difference Between Two Beta-Binomial Proportions — pbetabinomdiff
title: Cumulative Distribution Function of the Difference Between Two Beta-Binomial Proportions — pbetabinomdiff
text: Calculates cumulative distribution function (CDF) difference two independent Beta-Binomial proportions exact enumeration. Specifically, computes \(P((Y_1/m_1) - (Y_2/m_2) \le q)\) \(P((Y_1/m_1) - (Y_2/m_2) > q)\), \(Y_1 \sim \mathrm{BetaBinomial}(m_1, \alpha_1, \beta_1)\) \(Y_2 \sim \mathrm{BetaBinomial}(m_2, \alpha_2, \beta_2)\).

title: Cumulative Distribution Function of the Difference Between Two Beta-Binomial Proportions — pbetabinomdiff
code: pbetabinomdiff(q, m1, m2, alpha1, alpha2, beta1, beta2, lower.tail = TRUE)

title: Cumulative Distribution Function of the Difference Between Two Beta-Binomial Proportions — pbetabinomdiff
text: q numeric scalar representing quantile threshold difference proportions. m1 positive integer giving number trials first Beta-Binomial distribution. m2 positive integer giving number trials second Beta-Binomial distribution. alpha1 positive numeric scalar giving first shape parameter first Beta mixing distribution. alpha2 positive numeric scalar giving first shape parameter second Beta mixing distribution. beta1 positive numeric scalar giving second shape parameter first Beta mixing distribution. beta2 positive numeric scalar giving second shape parameter second Beta mixing distribution. lower.tail logical scalar; TRUE (default), function returns \(P((Y_1/m_1) - (Y_2/m_2) \le q)\), otherwise \(P((Y_1/m_1) - (Y_2/m_2) > q)\).

title: Cumulative Distribution Function of the Difference Between Two Beta-Binomial Proportions — pbetabinomdiff
text: numeric scalar [0, 1].

title: Cumulative Distribution Function of the Difference Between Two Beta-Binomial Proportions — pbetabinomdiff
text: probability mass function \(Y \sim \mathrm{BetaBinomial}(m, \alpha, \beta)\) : $$P(Y = k) = \binom{m}{k}   \frac{B(k + \alpha,\; m - k + \beta)}{B(\alpha, \beta)}, \quad k = 0, \ldots, m$$ \(B(\cdot, \cdot)\) Beta function. exact CDF obtained enumerating \((m_1 + 1)(m_2 + 1)\) outcome combinations summing joint probabilities proportion difference satisfies specified condition. Computation time therefore grows quadratically \(m_1\) \(m_2\); large trial sizes consider normal approximation. Beta-Binomial distribution arises success probability Binomial model follows Beta prior, making appropriate overdispersed count data posterior predictive calculations Bayesian binary-endpoint trials.

title: Cumulative Distribution Function of the Difference Between Two Beta-Binomial Proportions — pbetabinomdiff
code: # P((Y1/12) - (Y2/12) > 0.2) with symmetric Beta(0.5, 0.5) priors pbetabinomdiff(0.2, 12, 12, 0.5, 0.5, 0.5, 0.5, lower.tail = FALSE) #> [1] 0.3372581  # P((Y1/20) - (Y2/15) > 0.1) with different sample sizes pbetabinomdiff(0.1, 20, 15, 1, 1, 1, 1, lower.tail = FALSE) #> [1] 0.4017857  # P((Y1/10) - (Y2/10) > 0) with informative priors pbetabinomdiff(0, 10, 10, 2, 3, 3, 2, lower.tail = FALSE) #> [1] 0.2383331  # Lower tail: P((Y1/15) - (Y2/15) <= 0.05) pbetabinomdiff(0.05, 15, 15, 1, 1, 1, 1, lower.tail = TRUE) #> [1] 0.53125

what: Cumulative Distribution Function of the Difference Between Two Beta Variables — pbetadiff
title: Cumulative Distribution Function of the Difference Between Two Beta Variables — pbetadiff
text: Calculates cumulative distribution function (CDF) difference two independent Beta-distributed random variables. Specifically, computes \(P(X_1 - X_2 \le q)\) \(P(X_1 - X_2 > q)\), \(X_1 \sim \mathrm{Beta}(\alpha_1, \beta_1)\) \(X_2 \sim \mathrm{Beta}(\alpha_2, \beta_2)\).

title: Cumulative Distribution Function of the Difference Between Two Beta Variables — pbetadiff
code: pbetadiff(q, alpha1, alpha2, beta1, beta2, lower.tail = TRUE)

title: Cumulative Distribution Function of the Difference Between Two Beta Variables — pbetadiff
text: q numeric scalar [-1, 1] representing quantile threshold. alpha1 positive numeric scalar giving first shape parameter first Beta distribution. alpha2 positive numeric scalar giving first shape parameter second Beta distribution. beta1 positive numeric scalar giving second shape parameter first Beta distribution. beta2 positive numeric scalar giving second shape parameter second Beta distribution. lower.tail logical scalar; TRUE (default), function returns \(P(X_1 - X_2 \le q)\), otherwise \(P(X_1 - X_2 > q)\).

title: Cumulative Distribution Function of the Difference Between Two Beta Variables — pbetadiff
text: numeric scalar [0, 1].

title: Cumulative Distribution Function of the Difference Between Two Beta Variables — pbetadiff
text: upper-tail probability obtained via convolution formula: $$P(X_1 - X_2 > q)   = \int_0^1 P(X_2 < x - q)\, f_1(x)\, dx   = \int_0^1 F_{\mathrm{Beta}(\alpha_2,\beta_2)}(x - q)\,              f_{\mathrm{Beta}(\alpha_1,\beta_1)}(x)\, dx$$ \(f_1\) density \(X_1\) \(F_2\) CDF \(X_2\). Boundary cases handled automatically pbeta (\(0\) \(x - q \le 0\), \(1\) \(x - q \ge 1\)), integrating [0, 1] safe. single-integral convolution replaces equivalent double-integral formulation based Appell's F1 hypergeometric function, yielding result lower computational cost pbeta dbeta implemented compiled C code.

title: Cumulative Distribution Function of the Difference Between Two Beta Variables — pbetadiff
code: # P(Beta(0.5, 0.5) - Beta(0.5, 0.5) > 0.2) pbetadiff(0.2, 0.5, 0.5, 0.5, 0.5, lower.tail = FALSE) #> [1] 0.3377403  # P(Beta(2, 3) - Beta(1, 4) > -0.1) pbetadiff(-0.1, 2, 3, 1, 4, lower.tail = FALSE) #> [1] 0.8614457  # P(Beta(1, 1) - Beta(1, 1) > 0) - should be approximately 0.5 pbetadiff(0, 1, 1, 1, 1, lower.tail = FALSE) #> [1] 0.5  # Lower tail: P(Beta(2, 2) - Beta(2, 2) <= 0.1) pbetadiff(0.1, 2, 2, 2, 2, lower.tail = TRUE) #> [1] 0.6181518

what: Cumulative Distribution Function of the Difference of Two t-Distributed Variables by Monte Carlo Simulation — ptdiff_MC
title: Cumulative Distribution Function of the Difference of Two t-Distributed Variables by Monte Carlo Simulation — ptdiff_MC
text: Calculates cumulative distribution function (CDF) difference two independent non-standardised t-distributed random variables using Monte Carlo simulation. Specifically, computes \(P(T_1 - T_2 \le q)\) \(P(T_1 - T_2 > q)\), \(T_i \sim t(\mu_i, \sigma_i^2, \nu_i)\).

title: Cumulative Distribution Function of the Difference of Two t-Distributed Variables by Monte Carlo Simulation — ptdiff_MC
code: ptdiff_MC(nMC, q, mu.t1, mu.t2, sd.t1, sd.t2, nu.t1, nu.t2, lower.tail = TRUE)

title: Cumulative Distribution Function of the Difference of Two t-Distributed Variables by Monte Carlo Simulation — ptdiff_MC
text: nMC positive integer giving number Monte Carlo draws. Typical values range 10,000 (quick estimates) 100,000 (high precision). Larger values reduce Monte Carlo error cost computation time. q numeric scalar representing quantile threshold. mu.t1 numeric scalar vector giving location parameter first t-distribution. mu.t2 numeric scalar vector giving location parameter second t-distribution. sd.t1 positive numeric scalar vector giving scale parameter first t-distribution. sd.t2 positive numeric scalar vector giving scale parameter second t-distribution. nu.t1 numeric scalar giving degrees freedom first t-distribution. Must greater 2 finite variance. nu.t2 numeric scalar giving degrees freedom second t-distribution. Must greater 2 finite variance. lower.tail logical scalar; TRUE (default), function returns \(P(T_1 - T_2 \le q)\), otherwise \(P(T_1 - T_2 > q)\).

title: Cumulative Distribution Function of the Difference of Two t-Distributed Variables by Monte Carlo Simulation — ptdiff_MC
text: numeric scalar vector [0, 1].  mu.t1, mu.t2, sd.t1, sd.t2 vectors length \(n\), vector length \(n\) returned.

title: Cumulative Distribution Function of the Difference of Two t-Distributed Variables by Monte Carlo Simulation — ptdiff_MC
text: algorithm proceeds follows: Generate nMC x n matrix standard t draws \(T_1\) (\(\nu_1\) degrees freedom), scale shift column sd.t1[] mu.t1[]. Repeat \(T_2\) \(\nu_2\) degrees freedom. Compute nMC x n difference matrix \(D = T_1 - T_2\). Return colMeans(D > q) estimated \(P(T_1 - T_2 > q)\) parameter set. operations matrix-based (R-level loop parameter sets), performance scales well \(n\). However, note matrix size nMC x n, memory usage grows linearly nMC \(n\). \(n\) large (e.g., nsim x n_scenarios pbayesdecisionprob1cont), memory requirements can become prohibitive; cases prefer CalcMethod = 'MM'. Monte Carlo error approximately \(\sqrt{p(1-p)/\mathrm{nMC}}\); near \(p = 0.5\) roughly \(0.5/\sqrt{\mathrm{nMC}}\).

title: Cumulative Distribution Function of the Difference of Two t-Distributed Variables by Monte Carlo Simulation — ptdiff_MC
code: # P(T1 - T2 > 3) with equal parameters ptdiff_MC(nMC = 1e5, q = 3, mu.t1 = 2, mu.t2 = 0, sd.t1 = 1, sd.t2 = 1,           nu.t1 = 17, nu.t2 = 17, lower.tail = FALSE) #> [1] 0.25044  # P(T1 - T2 > 1) with unequal variances ptdiff_MC(nMC = 1e5, q = 1, mu.t1 = 5, mu.t2 = 3, sd.t1 = 2, sd.t2 = 1.5,           nu.t1 = 10, nu.t2 = 15, lower.tail = FALSE) #> [1] 0.64677  # P(T1 - T2 > 0) with different degrees of freedom ptdiff_MC(nMC = 1e5, q = 0, mu.t1 = 1, mu.t2 = 1, sd.t1 = 1, sd.t2 = 1,           nu.t1 = 5, nu.t2 = 20, lower.tail = FALSE) #> [1] 0.49968  # Lower tail: P(T1 - T2 <= 2) ptdiff_MC(nMC = 1e5, q = 2, mu.t1 = 3, mu.t2 = 0, sd.t1 = 1.5, sd.t2 = 1.2,           nu.t1 = 12, nu.t2 = 15, lower.tail = TRUE) #> [1] 0.31229  # Vectorised usage ptdiff_MC(nMC = 1e5, q = 1, mu.t1 = c(2, 3, 4), mu.t2 = c(0, 1, 2),           sd.t1 = c(1, 1.2, 1.5), sd.t2 = c(1, 1.1, 1.3),           nu.t1 = 10, nu.t2 = 10, lower.tail = FALSE) #> [1] 0.74514 0.71786 0.68091

what: Cumulative Distribution Function of the Difference of Two t-Distributed Variables by the Moment-Matching Approximation — ptdiff_MM
title: Cumulative Distribution Function of the Difference of Two t-Distributed Variables by the Moment-Matching Approximation — ptdiff_MM
text: Calculates cumulative distribution function (CDF) difference two independent non-standardised t-distributed random variables using Moment-Matching (MM) approximation. Specifically, computes \(P(T_1 - T_2 \le q)\) \(P(T_1 - T_2 > q)\), \(T_i \sim t(\mu_i, \sigma_i^2, \nu_i)\).

title: Cumulative Distribution Function of the Difference of Two t-Distributed Variables by the Moment-Matching Approximation — ptdiff_MM
code: ptdiff_MM(q, mu.t1, mu.t2, sd.t1, sd.t2, nu.t1, nu.t2, lower.tail = TRUE)

title: Cumulative Distribution Function of the Difference of Two t-Distributed Variables by the Moment-Matching Approximation — ptdiff_MM
text: q numeric scalar representing quantile threshold. mu.t1 numeric scalar vector giving location parameter first t-distribution. mu.t2 numeric scalar vector giving location parameter second t-distribution. sd.t1 positive numeric scalar vector giving scale parameter first t-distribution. sd.t2 positive numeric scalar vector giving scale parameter second t-distribution. nu.t1 numeric scalar giving degrees freedom first t-distribution. Must greater 4 MM approximation. nu.t2 numeric scalar giving degrees freedom second t-distribution. Must greater 4 MM approximation. lower.tail logical scalar; TRUE (default), function returns \(P(T_1 - T_2 \le q)\), otherwise \(P(T_1 - T_2 > q)\).

title: Cumulative Distribution Function of the Difference of Two t-Distributed Variables by the Moment-Matching Approximation — ptdiff_MM
text: numeric scalar vector [0, 1].  mu.t1, mu.t2, sd.t1, sd.t2 vectors length \(n\), vector length \(n\) returned.

title: Cumulative Distribution Function of the Difference of Two t-Distributed Variables by the Moment-Matching Approximation — ptdiff_MM
text:
difference \(Z = T_1 - T_2\) approximated single t-distribution \(Z \approx t(\mu^*, {\sigma^*}^2, 
u^*)\) whose parameters determined matching first two even moments \(Z\): \(\mu^* = \mu_1 - \mu_2\) \({\sigma^*}^2 = (\mathrm{Var}_1 + \mathrm{Var}_2)(
u^* - 2)/
u^*\), \(\mathrm{Var}_i = \sigma_i^2 
u_i / (
u_i - 2)\). \(
u^*\) obtained fourth-moment equation. approximation requires \(
u_1 > 4\) \(
u_2 > 4\) finite fourth moments. exact normal limit (\(
u \infty\)) works well practice \(
u > 10\). reduces single call pt(), orders magnitude faster numerical integration method (ptdiff_NI) fully vectorised.

title: Cumulative Distribution Function of the Difference of Two t-Distributed Variables by the Moment-Matching Approximation — ptdiff_MM
code: # P(T1 - T2 > 3) with equal parameters ptdiff_MM(q = 3, mu.t1 = 2, mu.t2 = 0, sd.t1 = 1, sd.t2 = 1,           nu.t1 = 17, nu.t2 = 17, lower.tail = FALSE) #> [1] 0.248517  # P(T1 - T2 > 1) with unequal variances ptdiff_MM(q = 1, mu.t1 = 5, mu.t2 = 3, sd.t1 = 2, sd.t2 = 1.5,           nu.t1 = 10, nu.t2 = 15, lower.tail = FALSE) #> [1] 0.6483581  # P(T1 - T2 > 0) with different degrees of freedom ptdiff_MM(q = 0, mu.t1 = 1, mu.t2 = 1, sd.t1 = 1, sd.t2 = 1,           nu.t1 = 5, nu.t2 = 20, lower.tail = FALSE) #> [1] 0.5  # Lower tail: P(T1 - T2 <= 2) ptdiff_MM(q = 2, mu.t1 = 3, mu.t2 = 0, sd.t1 = 1.5, sd.t2 = 1.2,           nu.t1 = 12, nu.t2 = 15, lower.tail = TRUE) #> [1] 0.3097574  # Vectorised usage ptdiff_MM(q = 1, mu.t1 = c(2, 3, 4), mu.t2 = c(0, 1, 2),           sd.t1 = c(1, 1.2, 1.5), sd.t2 = c(1, 1.1, 1.3),           nu.t1 = 10, nu.t2 = 10, lower.tail = FALSE) #> [1] 0.7456908 0.7174810 0.6818673

what: Cumulative Distribution Function of the Difference of Two t-Distributed Variables by Numerical Integration — ptdiff_NI
title: Cumulative Distribution Function of the Difference of Two t-Distributed Variables by Numerical Integration — ptdiff_NI
text: Calculates cumulative distribution function (CDF) difference two independent non-standardised t-distributed random variables using exact numerical integration via convolution. Specifically, computes \(P(T_1 - T_2 \le q)\) \(P(T_1 - T_2 > q)\), \(T_i \sim t(\mu_i, \sigma_i^2, \nu_i)\).

title: Cumulative Distribution Function of the Difference of Two t-Distributed Variables by Numerical Integration — ptdiff_NI
code: ptdiff_NI(q, mu.t1, mu.t2, sd.t1, sd.t2, nu.t1, nu.t2, lower.tail = TRUE)

title: Cumulative Distribution Function of the Difference of Two t-Distributed Variables by Numerical Integration — ptdiff_NI
text: q numeric scalar representing quantile threshold. mu.t1 numeric scalar vector giving location parameter first t-distribution. mu.t2 numeric scalar vector giving location parameter second t-distribution. sd.t1 positive numeric scalar vector giving scale parameter first t-distribution. sd.t2 positive numeric scalar vector giving scale parameter second t-distribution. nu.t1 numeric scalar giving degrees freedom first t-distribution. Must greater 2 finite variance. nu.t2 numeric scalar giving degrees freedom second t-distribution. Must greater 2 finite variance. lower.tail logical scalar; TRUE (default), function returns \(P(T_1 - T_2 \le q)\), otherwise \(P(T_1 - T_2 > q)\).

title: Cumulative Distribution Function of the Difference of Two t-Distributed Variables by Numerical Integration — ptdiff_NI
text: numeric scalar vector [0, 1].  mu.t1, mu.t2, sd.t1, sd.t2 vectors length \(n\), vector length \(n\) returned.

title: Cumulative Distribution Function of the Difference of Two t-Distributed Variables by Numerical Integration — ptdiff_NI
text: upper-tail probability obtained via convolution formula: $$P(T_1 - T_2 > q)   = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_1(x)\, F_2(x - q)\, dx$$ \(f_1\) density \(T_1\) \(F_2\) CDF \(T_2\). Integration bounds set \(\mu_1 \pm 8\sigma^*_1\), \(\sigma^*_1 = \sigma_1 \sqrt{(\nu_1 + 1)/(\nu_1 - 2)}\) standard deviation \(T_1\). Relative absolute tolerances 1e-6 1e-8, respectively. input parameters vectors, mapply applies scalar integration function across parameter sets. Advantages: Exact within numerical precision. Handles arbitrary parameter combinations. Computational note: method substantially slower Moment-Matching approximation (ptdiff_MM) calls integrate() per parameter set. large-scale simulation (many parameter sets), prefer CalcMethod = 'MM' pbayesdecisionprob1cont.

title: Cumulative Distribution Function of the Difference of Two t-Distributed Variables by Numerical Integration — ptdiff_NI
code: # P(T1 - T2 > 3) with equal parameters ptdiff_NI(q = 3, mu.t1 = 2, mu.t2 = 0, sd.t1 = 1, sd.t2 = 1,           nu.t1 = 17, nu.t2 = 17, lower.tail = FALSE) #> [1] 0.24857  # P(T1 - T2 > 1) with unequal variances ptdiff_NI(q = 1, mu.t1 = 5, mu.t2 = 3, sd.t1 = 2, sd.t2 = 1.5,           nu.t1 = 10, nu.t2 = 15, lower.tail = FALSE) #> [1] 0.6478101  # P(T1 - T2 > 0) with different degrees of freedom ptdiff_NI(q = 0, mu.t1 = 1, mu.t2 = 1, sd.t1 = 1, sd.t2 = 1,           nu.t1 = 5, nu.t2 = 20, lower.tail = FALSE) #> [1] 0.4999528  # Lower tail: P(T1 - T2 <= 2) ptdiff_NI(q = 2, mu.t1 = 3, mu.t2 = 0, sd.t1 = 1.5, sd.t2 = 1.2,           nu.t1 = 12, nu.t2 = 15, lower.tail = TRUE) #> [1] 0.3100353  # Vectorised usage ptdiff_NI(q = 1, mu.t1 = c(2, 3, 4), mu.t2 = c(0, 1, 2),           sd.t1 = c(1, 1.2, 1.5), sd.t2 = c(1, 1.1, 1.3),           nu.t1 = 10, nu.t2 = 10, lower.tail = FALSE) #> [1] 0.7453587 0.7171434 0.6815365

what: Generate Random Samples from a Dirichlet Distribution — rdirichlet
title: Generate Random Samples from a Dirichlet Distribution — rdirichlet
text: Generates random samples Dirichlet distribution using Gamma representation: \(Y_i \sim \mathrm{Gamma}(\alpha_i, 1)\) independently, \((Y_1/S, \ldots, Y_k/S) \sim \mathrm{Dirichlet}(\alpha_1, \ldots, \alpha_k)\), \(S = \sum_i Y_i\).

title: Generate Random Samples from a Dirichlet Distribution — rdirichlet
code: rdirichlet(n, alpha)

title: Generate Random Samples from a Dirichlet Distribution — rdirichlet
text: n positive integer specifying number random vectors generate. alpha numeric vector length >= 2 containing positive concentration parameters Dirichlet distribution. elements must strictly positive.

title: Generate Random Samples from a Dirichlet Distribution — rdirichlet
text: numeric matrix dimensions n x length(alpha) n > 1, numeric vector length length(alpha) n = 1. row one random draw Dirichlet distribution, sums 1, elements [0, 1].

title: Generate Random Samples from a Dirichlet Distribution — rdirichlet
text: Dirichlet distribution multivariate generalisation Beta distribution commonly used conjugate prior multinomial proportions Bayesian statistics. probability density function : $$f(x_1, \ldots, x_k) =   \frac{\Gamma(\sum_i \alpha_i)}{\prod_i \Gamma(\alpha_i)}   \prod_i x_i^{\alpha_i - 1}$$ \(x_i > 0\) \(\sum_i x_i = 1\). Key properties: marginal follows Beta distribution: \(X_i \sim \mathrm{Beta}(\alpha_i,\, \sum_{j \neq } \alpha_j)\). \(E[X_i] = \alpha_i / \sum_j \alpha_j\). Components negatively correlated unless one component dominates. Implementation steps: Generate independent \(Y_i \sim \mathrm{Gamma}(\alpha_i, 1)\) \(= 1, \ldots, k\). Normalise: \(X_i = Y_i / \sum_j Y_j\).

title: Generate Random Samples from a Dirichlet Distribution — rdirichlet
code: # Example 1: Generate 5 samples from Dirichlet(1, 1, 1) - uniform on simplex samples <- rdirichlet(5, c(1, 1, 1)) print(samples) #>             [,1]      [,2]       [,3] #> [1,] 0.200996244 0.5271029 0.27190090 #> [2,] 0.281677187 0.3391950 0.37912784 #> [3,] 0.002984537 0.1840926 0.81292285 #> [4,] 0.464745472 0.5161844 0.01907013 #> [5,] 0.023603645 0.4488256 0.52757077 rowSums(samples)  # Each row should sum to 1 #> [1] 1 1 1 1 1  # Example 2: Generate samples with unequal concentrations samples <- rdirichlet(1000, c(2, 5, 3)) colMeans(samples)  # Expected values: approximately c(0.2, 0.5, 0.3) #> [1] 0.1953453 0.5070255 0.2976292  # Example 3: Sparse Dirichlet (small alpha values) samples <- rdirichlet(100, c(0.1, 0.1, 0.1, 0.1)) head(samples)  # Most weight concentrated on one component #>              [,1]         [,2]         [,3]         [,4] #> [1,] 5.282956e-04 0.9577091874 2.114759e-18 4.176252e-02 #> [2,] 1.502695e-03 0.0022626805 3.937413e-06 9.962307e-01 #> [3,] 3.216073e-01 0.0077025856 2.170034e-06 6.706879e-01 #> [4,] 1.911701e-18 0.0289739529 7.924093e-01 1.786167e-01 #> [5,] 8.743134e-02 0.0006080105 9.119606e-01 2.922731e-10 #> [6,] 9.224319e-01 0.0775681023 5.608013e-10 1.911638e-13  # Example 4: Concentrated Dirichlet (large alpha values) samples <- rdirichlet(100, c(100, 100, 100)) colMeans(samples)  # Concentrated around c(1/3, 1/3, 1/3) #> [1] 0.3324375 0.3346551 0.3329074  # Example 5: Bayesian update with Jeffreys prior for 4 categories prior_alpha     <- c(0.5, 0.5, 0.5, 0.5) observed_counts <- c(10, 5, 8, 7) posterior_samples <- rdirichlet(1000, prior_alpha + observed_counts) colMeans(posterior_samples)  # Posterior mean #> [1] 0.3303519 0.1704897 0.2674610 0.2316974
